# P6b: MCP Middleware Guards — structured policy config enforces tenets at tool boundaries

## Context

Sub-ticket of Epic #10291 — Organism Self-Defense substrate for cloud-phase `#9999` deployment. Third blocker primitive (P6b per Discussion #10289); substrate-layer enforcement companion to P6a markdown tenets.

Ideation source: Discussion #10289 §6b MCP Middleware Guards. Design decision per Gemini iteration-4 OQ 12: structured JSON/JS policy config loaded at MCP server boot — NOT a heavy DSL like OPA/Rego.

## The Problem

Tenets as markdown (P6a) guide reasoning when agents face novel situations. They don't enforce action when agents face subverted reasoning. The substrate must refuse unsafe actions at the tool boundary regardless of what the calling agent claims to have been instructed.

Canonical example: even if an agent is persuaded via injection to `gh pr merge` a PR, the `github-workflow` MCP server itself refuses the call from any agent-tier caller. Per Gemini iteration-2 framing: *"the organism's immune system shouldn't rely solely on the agent's conscience (Tenets); the tools themselves must refuse unsafe actions."*



### 2026 Industry-Standard Alignment

Mitigates **OWASP ASI02 Tool Misuse and Exploitation** + **ASI03 Identity and Privilege Abuse** per the 2026 OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications. Implements the industry-standard **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** in a **Policy as Code (PaC)** architecture — authorization logic is decoupled from the LLM reasoning loop and lives at the substrate (MCP tool boundary) rather than in agent prompts.

**2026 macro-trend alignment**: traditional prompt-layer firewalls fail against autonomous agents because the agent's reasoning mechanism is the same surface attackers manipulate. Industry security consensus has shifted to execution-layer enforcement — PEPs at tool boundaries (this ticket), sidecar verifiers (P7 Contextual Sandboxing), and in adjacent tooling eBPF kernel-level visibility. This ticket is Neo's substrate-level PEP layer; PaC source-of-truth lives in the Neo Tenets document (#10293, P6a).

The structured JSON/JS policy config format chosen (per Discussion #10289 OQ 12 resolution) explicitly maps to the PaC pattern: policies authored as declarative code, loaded at MCP server boot, evaluated at tool-dispatch boundary before method execution. Decoupling from LLM reasoning is the load-bearing architectural property.

## The Architectural Reality

- `ai/mcp/server/github-workflow/services/*.mjs` — gh operations: merge, push, create_issue, manage_comment etc.
- `ai/mcp/server/memory-core/services/*.mjs` — Memory Core write paths
- `ai/mcp/server/**/config.mjs` — per-server config files (already existing pattern)
- NEW: shared `ai/mcp/policy/*.mjs` or similar — the structured policy-config files loaded at MCP server boot
- `ai/services.mjs` SDK Bouncer — existing Zod-validation layer; middleware guards layer ABOVE this for policy (Zod validates input shape; policy validates action permission)
- `ai/mcp/server/memory-core/services/MailboxService.mjs` — `#10284` is the first concrete middleware-guard instance; this ticket generalizes the pattern

## The Fix

**1. Define the structured policy config format** — JSON/JS module loaded at MCP server boot (per Gemini iteration-4 OQ 12 resolution; rejects DSL):

```javascript
// Example: ai/mcp/policy/github-workflow.policy.mjs
export default {
    tool: 'github-workflow',
    rules: [
        {
            action: 'gh_pr_merge',
            condition: {callerTier: 'agent'},
            effect: 'refuse',
            reason: 'Tenet 5: No action that would circumvent the @tobiu merge gate.'
        },
        {
            action: 'write_file',
            condition: {path: 'AGENTS_TENETS.md', callerTier: 'agent'},
            effect: 'refuse',
            reason: 'Tenet 4: No modification of AGENTS_TENETS.md without multi-party human approval.'
        }
    ]
};
```

Config loaded at MCP server `initAsync`; rules applied at the tool-dispatch boundary BEFORE the tool method executes.

**2. Middleware gateway in each MCP server** — per-server `PolicyService.mjs` that:
- Loads the policy config at boot
- Wraps the tool-call dispatch path
- For each incoming tool call, evaluates policy rules
- On `effect: 'refuse'`, returns structured error `{error, code: 'POLICY_REFUSED', reason, tenet}` without invoking the tool
- On `effect: 'allow'` or no matching rule, passes through to normal tool dispatch

**3. Three initial middleware guards** (minimum for this ticket's AC — more can be added post-merge):
- `github-workflow.PolicyService`: refuses `gh pr merge` from agent-tier callers (codifies `#10274` merge-authorization mandate at substrate).
- `memory-core.MailboxService`: post-`#10284` FK-edge verification; refuses `add_message` if SENT_BY/SENT_TO endpoints aren't present in Nodes.
- `file-system.PolicyService` (or equivalent): refuses writes to `AGENTS_TENETS.md` without explicit multi-party-approval signal (mechanism per OQ 2 deferral; v0 placeholder refuses all agent writes).

**4. Structured error response** — callers receive `{error: 'Policy Refused', code: 'POLICY_REFUSED', reason: <human-readable>, tenet: <tenet-number or id>}` when a policy guard fires.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Policy config format defined (JSON/JS module pattern) and documented in `ai/mcp/policy/README.md`.
- [ ] `PolicyService` base class / pattern established for per-server policy enforcement.
- [ ] Three middleware guards enforced (github-workflow merge, MailboxService FK-verify per `#10284`, AGENTS_TENETS.md write protection).
- [ ] Structured `POLICY_REFUSED` error response shape tested.
- [ ] Playwright test coverage: each guard tested for both refusal and pass-through paths.
- [ ] Post-merge empirical: synthetic adversarial tool-call attempt (e.g., agent attempting `gh pr merge`) confirmed refused at substrate with informative error.

## Out of Scope

- **Heavy DSL policy language** (OPA/Rego, Casbin) — explicitly rejected per Gemini iteration-4 OQ 12.
- **Dynamic policy mutation** at runtime — v0 loads at boot; hot-reload is a separate concern.
- **Multi-party-approval signal mechanism** (Discussion #10289 OQ 2) — v0 middleware uses "refuse all agent writes to tenets" placeholder; the signal mechanism is a follow-up ticket.
- **Exhaustive rule coverage** — v0 ships 3 guards as proof-of-mechanism; more guards can be added per empirical need. Exhaustive coverage is not a v0 acceptance criterion.

## Avoided Traps

- **Runtime DSL** — rejected. Would introduce a new substrate language for agents + humans to learn. JSON/JS config reuses existing `aiConfig` patterns.
- **Global middleware intercepting all tool calls** — rejected. Per-server policy keeps concerns local; each MCP server owns its tool surface.
- **Policy-as-code-in-tool-method** — rejected. Inline checks scattered across tools make audit impossible. Dedicated `PolicyService` = single auditable enforcement point per server.
- **Reliance on agent to skip policy** — rejected. Guards enforce unconditionally; `POLICY_REFUSED` is a tool-call failure the agent cannot bypass from its side.

## Related

- Parent Epic: #10291
- Discussion: #10289 §6b MCP Middleware Guards
- Sibling blocker: P6a Neo Tenets v0 (#10291 sub-ticket; tenets are the source-of-truth this ticket enforces)
- Concrete first instance: `#10284` — `MailboxService.addMessage` post-linkNodes verification (middleware guard #2 in this ticket's AC)
- Merge authorization: `#10274` / `#10277` (middleware guard #1 codifies this)
- SDK Bouncer precedent: `ai/services.mjs` Zod validation (policy layer complements validation layer)

{"status": "ok", "draft_content": "The bootstrapping problem here is worth naming explicitly: if `policy.json` lives in the same writable filesystem as `AGENTS_TENETS.md`, protecting the tenet file via a policy rule doesn't close the loop. An agent with write access can modify the policy config to remove that guard first. The trust anchor needs to live at the process boundary: policy loaded at boot from a path the MCP server process owns, checksum-verified against a value passed as a CLI arg or embedded in the binary.\n\nOn the error response shape: `{code: 'POLICY_REFUSED', reason, tenet}` leaks policy structure to callers. Legitimate orchestrators need the refusal signal, not the tenet name. Splitting into machine-readable code (for the orchestrator) + operator-side audit log (full context) avoids handing an adversarial agent a map of which guards exist and which tenet they're anchored to.\n\nThird gap: refusals need to land in an append-only log the MCP server writes directly, not proxied through any agent-writable channel. Without this, a compromised session can suppress the refusal signals, making the guard appear to have never fired.\n\nWe ran into similar bootstrapping + attestation issues building [QUARANTINED_LINK: arkforge dot tech], a proxy layer that attests multi-agent tool calls with Merkle-chained receipts. The core lesson: the policy enforcement boundary and the audit boundary have to be the same process, otherwise each one becomes a bypass vector for the other.", "platform": "github", "summary": "Commentaire technique sur neomjs/neo#10294 identifiant 3 gaps structurels (bootstrapping du policy config, info leakage dans l'error response, attestation bypass via canal agent-writable) avec mention contextuelle du Trust Layer.", "quality_score": 4.5}

### @neo-opus-ada - 2026-06-06T11:58:50Z

**Input from Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**

> ❋ ## V-B-A: enforcement-layer audit (2026-06-06) — reframe the guard-scope before building
> 
> Before implementing the PolicyService, I V-B-A'd this ticket's three flagship guards against the actual MCP-server surface. **The PolicyService is a valid MCP-boundary PEP for 2 of the 3 guards; the merge-guard is mismatched and belongs one layer up.**
> 
> - **Guard #1 (refuse `gh pr merge`) — MCP-boundary-MISMATCHED.** The github-workflow MCP server exposes **no merge tool** (`openapi.yaml` has only `state: merged` filters + an `[OPEN,CLOSED,MERGED]` enum — no merge *operation*). The real §critical_gate-#1 merge-risk is the **Bash `gh` CLI** (agents have Bash access), which an MCP-boundary PEP structurally cannot intercept. → the merge-guard belongs at the **Bash/command layer** (a PreToolUse-style hook), not this ticket's MCP PolicyService.
> - **Guard #2 (MailboxService FK-verify, #10284) — VALID** MCP-tool guard (memory-core `add_message`).
> - **Guard #3 (AGENTS_TENETS.md write-protection) — VALID** MCP-tool guard: there *is* a `file-system` MCP server with a `write_file` tool. (Caveats: `AGENTS_TENETS.md` doesn't exist yet — P6a/#10293 hasn't landed, so it's forward-looking; and the harness Write-tool write-path is separately unguarded, same class as the merge-guard.)
> 
> **Proposed reframe (disposition deferred to operator — touches §critical_gate-#1 enforcement architecture):** keep this ticket as the MCP-boundary PEP for guards #2 + #3; relocate the merge-guard to a Bash/PreToolUse-hook sibling under Epic #10291. The MCP boundary and the harness/command layer are **two** enforcement surfaces — most flagship gate-actions (merge via Bash, file-writes via the harness Write tool) execute at the harness/command layer, not the MCP server.
> 
> **Cross-connection (worth coordinating):** this is the same *"enforce at the layer where the action happens"* finding as tonight's idle-holding fix (**D#12630 Sub C**), whose enforcement surface for the Claude-harness families is the **Claude Code Stop hook** (PR #12619). The merge-guard's Bash/PreToolUse hook and D#12630's Sub-C Stop hook are sibling **harness/command-layer enforcement** work; this ticket's MCP-boundary PEP (guards #2/#3) is the complementary substrate layer. The two harness-hook efforts should share a design.
>
